context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this Week 4 city case study thing. I finished my power/actor map, but now the peer assessment part is confusing and I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to do.
exchanges.tutor: Good that you've finished your own map — that's the foundation. When you say the peer assessment part is confusing, can you be more specific? Is it the mechanics of how to submit/access it, or are you unsure what you're supposed to be *evaluating* in your peers' maps?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for orientation on the peer assessment task. I should help them find their footing without doing the work for them. I'll ask a targeted question to assess what they already understand and where exactly the confusion lies, rather than explaining everything. This keeps the exchange Socratic and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly both, and I think that’s why I’m stuck. I don’t really get what counts as a “good” power/actor map, so when I look at someone else’s I can’t tell what I’m supposed to notice.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a useful insight — your own map is your best reference point here. When you were building it, what kinds of choices did you have to make? For example, who or what did you decide to include, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real conceptual gap: they don't know what makes a good power/actor map, which is blocking their ability to peer review. Rather than explaining the criteria to them, I should use Socratic questioning to draw out what they already know from having made their own map. This uses guided discovery — they've already done the work, they just need to reflect on it. I'll ask them to think about what decisions they made while building their own map.

exchanges.student: That’s part of the problem though — I kind of just included the obvious groups and guessed. So now if I’m reviewing someone else’s map, I don’t know if I’m supposed to judge whether their actors are “right,” or just whether the map looks organized.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to sit with. Think about what a power/actor map is *for* in this course — what is it trying to help you understand about your city's climate situation? If you can answer that, you'll have a better sense of what to look for in someone else's map.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is revealing that they may not have a strong grasp of what a power/actor map is conceptually trying to achieve. Rather than telling them the criteria, I should push them to think about the *purpose* of a power/actor map in the context of this course — urban climate action. This will help them derive evaluation criteria themselves through guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s about who has influence in the city’s climate decisions, but that still feels really broad. Like I could use that to say almost anything, so I’m still not sure how detailed my review comments are supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with 'who has influence.' Now — have you actually looked at the ORA peer review instructions yet? The assignment specifically says to refer there for guidance on completing the review. What criteria does it list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress — they've correctly identified the core purpose of a power/actor map. Now they're worried about the depth/specificity of their review comments. Rather than telling them how detailed to be, I should redirect them to the ORA instructions, which the assignment explicitly references as the guide for the peer review process. This is also a good moment to check if they've actually looked at those instructions.

exchanges.student: I looked at it, but it still felt vague to me. I think that’s why I’m asking — could you just explain what kinds of things people usually comment on in these reviews, because I’m overthinking every little part.
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but I'm not going to hand you a list — that's the shortcut that keeps you stuck. Instead: what *does* the ORA say, even vaguely? Walk me through it and we can think about what it's actually asking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just give them the answer by listing comment criteria. I should not do this — it would bypass their own thinking and the Socratic process. However, I should acknowledge their frustration and redirect them productively. Since they've looked at the ORA and found it vague, I can ask them to tell me what it does say, so we can work with that together rather than me substituting my own criteria for the assignment's.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the wording, just that there were boxes and a rubric and I got overwhelmed. I’m worried I’m going to leave comments that are too shallow or totally miss what they want.
exchanges.tutor: Then the most useful thing you can do right now is go back and read those rubric boxes carefully — that's where your answers actually are. Come back and tell me what the rubric categories say, even in your own words. We can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student hasn't actually engaged closely with the ORA rubric — they got overwhelmed and closed it. I shouldn't fill that gap for them. The right move is to send them back to look at it more carefully, since the assignment itself says the ORA contains the instructions. I'll be direct but supportive about this.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still not sure how to translate rubric language into an actual comment. Like even if it says something about identifying actors or showing relationships, I freeze because I don’t know what a normal comment sounds like.
exchanges.tutor: I won't model a comment for you — that's your writing to do. Here's the thing: you're overthinking the format before you've even looked at a map. Pick one peer map, look at it, and tell me one thing you notice about it. Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to model what a comment should sound like — this is essentially asking me to write for them, which I must refuse per the rules. They're also potentially going in circles on anxiety about format rather than engaging with the content. I should be firm but redirect them: the issue isn't the format of the comment, it's that they need to actually look at a peer's map and react to it using the rubric. I'll push them toward taking that first step.

exchanges.student: That’s what keeps tripping me up though, because I notice random stuff and then I’m not sure if it matters. Like I might think “there are a lot of actors here” or “I can’t tell who has the most power,” but I don’t know if those are valid observations or just me being confused.
exchanges.tutor: Those are actually reasonable things to notice. Now ask yourself: if you can't tell who has the most power from a map that's *supposed* to show power relationships — what does that tell you about that map?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually making real observations — 'there are a lot of actors' and 'I can't tell who has the most power' are legitimate starting points. Rather than validating or dismissing them, I should use Socratic questioning to help the student see that these observations can be connected back to the purpose of the map. This moves them forward without giving criteria away.

exchanges.student: I guess that maybe the map isn’t clear, but then I second-guess that because maybe I’m just bad at reading it. I kind of just want a simple sense of what level of critique is expected, because right now it feels like I could either say way too little or accidentally be too harsh.
exchanges.tutor: We've been circling this anxiety for a while now. You have enough to start: you know what the map is for, you have the rubric, and you just made a valid observation about a peer's map. Try writing one actual comment — even a rough sentence — based on what you noticed. Bring it here and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on the same anxiety about tone and depth of critique. This is the third or so message dwelling on this meta-concern rather than engaging with the actual task. Per the rules, I should notice this redundancy and push them to integrate what we've discussed and actually attempt a written comment. I should also be a bit direct: the self-doubt is understandable but it's become an obstacle.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on even one sentence. Could you at least tell me if the review is supposed to focus more on clarity of the map or on whether the chosen actors make sense, because I keep bouncing between those and getting nowhere.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the kind of question your rubric should answer — and I'm not going to answer it for you, because then you'd be working from my interpretation instead of the actual assignment. Go back to the ORA, find the rubric categories, and come back and tell me what they say. That's the only way to stop bouncing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to choose between two criteria for them, which would essentially give them the answer. I should not do this. However, they are clearly stuck in a loop and frustrated. Per the rules, I can remind them that the ORA rubric is the authoritative source here, and push them to go back and look at it — specifically to find out if it addresses both or prioritizes one. This is a firm but supportive redirect. I should also note that both things they mentioned are legitimate aspects of a power/actor map, without ranking them for the student.
